title: SwiGLU
description: A gated FFN design that splits the hidden expansion into value and gate branches, then uses SiLU to scale the result.
openingSummary: SwiGLU splits the FFN expansion into value and gate branches, uses SiLU on the gate, and multiplies them together before projection so the block can choose which hidden features stay strong.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: SwiGLU is a feed-forward variant used in many modern language models. Instead of one expanded hidden branch, the FFN creates two branches after attention: a value branch and a gate branch. The gate branch passes through a SiLU activation, then multiplies the value branch element by element before the block projects back to model width.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: SwiGLU optimizes for a more selective dense hidden path. Instead of treating every hidden feature the same way, it lets a learned gate strengthen or suppress each feature before the final projection.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: This gives many modern stacks a stronger dense FFN without changing the overall transformer block layout. For a reader, that means the page is best understood as a variant of the standard FFN, not as a separate module family.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The token state enters two learned projections. One branch carries candidate values. The other branch becomes a SiLU gate. The two branches multiply elementwise, then the block projects the result back to model width. The slot stays dense because every token still uses the same shared block.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: The dense baseline below shows the simpler one-branch FFN. The SwiGLU formula shows the extra gate that turns the same post-attention slot into a two-branch gated path.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: SwiGLU stays closer to the dense FFN baseline than MoE does. It changes how features are filtered inside one dense block rather than changing the slot into a sparse routed expert system.

variantsAndNearbyModules.title: Variants And Nearby Modules
variantsAndNearbyModules.body: These nearby pages show the dense FFN baseline, the broader FFN family context, and the sparse expert alternative that readers often compare against SwiGLU.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Many recent decoder-only language models use gated FFN designs like SwiGLU while keeping the same attention-then-FFN block structure.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: SwiGLU is still a dense block, so every token activates the whole path. It improves selectivity inside the FFN, but it does not add sparse expert capacity the way MoE does.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: SwiGLU appears often enough in modern model descriptions that readers benefit from seeing it as one of the default FFN baselines, not as a rare paper-only detail.

computeFlow.alt: SwiGLU graph showing value and gate branches, SiLU on the gate branch, and elementwise multiplication before projection
computeFlow.caption: SwiGLU changes the standard FFN by splitting the wide hidden state into value and gate branches before multiplying them back together

comparisonTable.caption: How SwiGLU compares with nearby FFN variants

swiglu.pathShape: Value branch multiplied by a SiLU gate branch
swiglu.activeCompute: All hidden units plus a learned gate
swiglu.mainTradeoff: More selective dense features, but still full dense compute

standard.pathShape: One dense expand -> activate -> project path
standard.activeCompute: All hidden units in one shared MLP
standard.mainTradeoff: Simpler path, but less selective hidden filtering

moe.pathShape: Router selects a few expert MLPs, then merges them
moe.activeCompute: Only top-k experts activate for each token
moe.mainTradeoff: More capacity, but routing and sparse training become part of the design

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

w1.definition: Projection into the wide hidden layer.

sigma.definition: Pointwise activation inside the dense hidden layer.

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

wv.definition: Projection that builds the value branch.

wg.definition: Projection that builds the gate branch.

wo.definition: Projection back to model width after gating.

odot.definition: Elementwise multiplication between the value and gate branches.
